module.title: LOOPS & RANGES
module.subtitle: numeric for · while · repeat · accumulation
module.theory:
## Doing something many times
Up to now your code ran each line once, top to bottom. A **loop** lets you repeat a block — count from 1 to 10, add up a list, keep going until a condition is met. This sector covers Lua's three loops and the patterns you build with them.

## The numeric `for` — a counted range
The most common loop walks a **range** of numbers. You give a **start**, an **end**, and (optionally) a **step**.

~~~lua
for i = 1, 5 do      -- i takes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
  print(i)
end
~~~

**WHAT** — `for i = a, b do ... end` runs the block once for each value of `i` from `a` up to and *including* `b`. **WHY** — when you know exactly how many times to repeat (or which numbers to visit), this is the clearest tool.

> INTEL — the end value is **inclusive**. `for i = 1, 5` really does run with `i = 5` on the last pass. This trips up people coming from languages where the end is excluded.

### Adding a step
A third number is the **step** — how much `i` changes each pass. A negative step counts **down**.

~~~lua
for i = 0, 10, 2 do print(i) end   -- 0 2 4 6 8 10
for i = 5, 1, -1 do print(i) end   -- 5 4 3 2 1
~~~

> WARNING — to count downward you MUST give a negative step. `for i = 5, 1 do` uses the default step of `+1`, sees that 5 is already past 1, and runs **zero** times — no error, just nothing happens. Write `for i = 5, 1, -1 do`.

## Accumulating — sum and product
The killer move of a loop is the **accumulator**: a variable you set up *before* the loop and update *inside* it. To total a list, start at `0` and add each value. To multiply, start at `1` and multiply.

~~~lua
local sum = 0
for i = 1, 4 do
  sum = sum + i      -- 0+1, then +2, +3, +4
end
print(sum)           -- 10
~~~

> WARNING — declare the accumulator OUTSIDE the loop. If you write `local sum = 0` *inside* the `do ... end`, it resets to 0 on every pass and your total is wrong. And remember Lua has no `++`: write `sum = sum + i`, never `sum++`.

> INTEL — pick the right seed. Sums start at **0** (adding 0 changes nothing). Products start at **1** (multiplying by 1 changes nothing). Seeding a product with 0 would force the answer to 0 forever.

## The `while` loop — repeat as long as a condition holds
When you DON'T know the count up front — "keep going until the number drops below 1" — use **while**. It checks the condition *first*, then runs the block, then checks again.

~~~lua
local n = 16
local steps = 0
while n > 1 do
  n = n / 2          -- you must change n, or this never ends
  steps = steps + 1
end
print(steps)         -- 4
~~~

> WARNING — a `while` runs forever unless something inside it eventually makes the condition false. The classic bug is forgetting to update the variable you're testing — an **infinite loop**. Always make sure the block moves toward the exit.

## The `repeat ... until` loop — run first, check after
`repeat ... until cond` is `while`'s mirror image: it runs the block **once before** checking, then keeps going **until** the condition becomes true. Because the body runs first, it always executes at least once.

~~~lua
local i = 1
repeat
  print(i)
  i = i + 1
until i > 3          -- prints 1, 2, 3
~~~

> INTEL — note the logic is flipped: `while cond` loops *while true*, but `repeat ... until cond` loops *until true* (i.e. while the condition is still false). Read the word `until` literally: "keep going until this becomes true."

## Building a result table with `table.insert`
Loops often produce a **list**. Start with an empty table `{}` and push values onto the end with `table.insert`. Lua arrays are **1-indexed**, and `table.insert(t, v)` appends `v` at position `#t + 1`.

~~~lua
local squares = {}
for i = 1, 4 do
  table.insert(squares, i * i)   -- 1, 4, 9, 16
end
print(squares[1], squares[4])    -- 1   16
print(#squares)                  -- 4
~~~

**WHAT** — `table.insert(t, v)` grows the array by one, putting `v` last. **WHY** — you rarely know the final size ahead of time; inserting lets the list grow as the loop runs. `#t` then tells you how many items landed.

## `break` — leave a loop early
Sometimes you want to stop the moment you find what you're after. **break** jumps straight out of the nearest loop, skipping the rest.

~~~lua
local found = nil
for i = 2, 100 do
  if 84 % i == 0 then
    found = i        -- first divisor of 84 that is >= 2
    break            -- stop looking
  end
end
print(found)         -- 2
~~~

> INTEL — `break` only exits the **innermost** loop it sits in. There is no `break 2` to escape several loops at once — for that you restructure with a flag or a helper function.

> WARNING — `%` is the **remainder** operator: `a % b == 0` means `b` divides `a` evenly. It's the standard "is this divisible / is this even" test — `n % 2 == 0` is true for even numbers.

exercises.title: RANGE TOTAL
exercises.brief: Sum 1 up to n with a numeric for.
exercises.prompt:
Define **sum_to(n)** that returns the total of every whole number from **1** to **n** (inclusive). Use a numeric `for` and an accumulator that starts at 0.
~~~lua
sum_to(4)   -- 10   (1 + 2 + 3 + 4)
sum_to(1)   -- 1
~~~
exercises.starter:
function sum_to(n)
  -- TODO: add 1, 2, ..., n into a running total
  return 0
end
exercises.solution:
function sum_to(n)
  local sum = 0
  for i = 1, n do
    sum = sum + i
  end
  return sum
end
exercises.hint: local sum = 0 before the loop; for i = 1, n do sum = sum + i end; then return sum.
exercises.lore: Walk the range one node at a time. The total is the toll you pay.

exercises.title: DESCENT VECTOR
exercises.brief: Count down from n into a list.
exercises.prompt:
Define **countdown(n)** that returns an array holding the numbers **n, n-1, ... , 1** in that order. Use a numeric `for` with a negative step and `table.insert`.
~~~lua
local t = countdown(3)
print(t[1], t[2], t[3])  -- 3  2  1
~~~
exercises.solution:
function countdown(n)
  local out = {}
  for i = n, 1, -1 do
    table.insert(out, i)
  end
  return out
end

tests.name: counts down from 3
tests.code:
local t = countdown(3)
assert(#t == 3 and t[1] == 3 and t[2] == 2 and t[3] == 1)

tests.name: single element
tests.code:
local t = countdown(1)
assert(#t == 1 and t[1] == 1)

tests.name: length matches n
tests.code:
local t = countdown(5)
assert(#t == 5 and t[1] == 5 and t[5] == 1)

exercises.hint: for i = n, 1, -1 do table.insert(out, i) end — the -1 step counts downward.
exercises.lore: Ten to one, the descent burns clean. Forget the minus and you never leave the pad.

exercises.title: CHAIN MULTIPLY
exercises.prompt:
Define **product_to(n)** that returns the product of every whole number from **1** to **n** (this is n factorial). Seed the accumulator at **1**, not 0.
~~~lua
product_to(4)   -- 24   (1 * 2 * 3 * 4)
product_to(1)   -- 1
~~~
exercises.starter:
function product_to(n)
  -- TODO: multiply 1 * 2 * ... * n (start the accumulator at 1)
  return 0
end
exercises.solution:
function product_to(n)
  local prod = 1
  for i = 1, n do
    prod = prod * i
  end
  return prod
end
exercises.hint: Start local prod = 1 (a 0 seed would zero everything); then prod = prod * i in the loop.
exercises.lore: Stack the multipliers. Seed it with zero and the whole chain flatlines.

exercises.title: EVEN HARVEST
exercises.brief: Collect even numbers up to n.
exercises.prompt:
Define **evens_to(n)** that returns an array of every even number from **2** up to **n** (inclusive), in ascending order. A `for` with step 2 makes this clean; build the list with `table.insert`.
~~~lua
local t = evens_to(8)
print(t[1], t[4])  -- 2  8
~~~
exercises.solution:
function evens_to(n)
  local out = {}
  for i = 2, n, 2 do
    table.insert(out, i)
  end
  return out
end

tests.name: evens up to 8
tests.code:
local t = evens_to(8)
assert(#t == 4 and t[1] == 2 and t[2] == 4 and t[3] == 6 and t[4] == 8)

tests.name: odd bound stops early
tests.code:
local t = evens_to(7)
assert(#t == 3 and t[3] == 6)

exercises.hint: for i = 2, n, 2 do table.insert(out, i) end — the step of 2 skips the odds.
exercises.lore: Skim every second packet off the wire. The grid breathes in twos.

exercises.title: HALVING CASCADE
exercises.prompt:
Define **halvings(n)** that returns how many times you can integer-halve **n** before it reaches **1** or below. Use a `while` loop: keep dividing while `n > 1`, counting each step. Use `//` (floor division) so the result stays a whole number.
~~~lua
halvings(16)  -- 4   (16 -> 8 -> 4 -> 2 -> 1)
halvings(1)   -- 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
function halvings(n)
  local steps = 0
  -- TODO: while n > 1, halve n (use //) and count the step
  return steps
end
exercises.solution:
function halvings(n)
  local steps = 0
  while n > 1 do
    n = n // 2
    steps = steps + 1
  end
  return steps
end
exercises.hint: while n > 1 do n = n // 2; steps = steps + 1 end — you must change n or it loops forever.
exercises.lore: Fold the signal in half, again and again, until only the core bit remains.

exercises.title: FIRST FACTOR
exercises.brief: Find the smallest divisor, then break.
exercises.prompt:
Define **first_divisor(n)** that returns the smallest integer **d** with `d >= 2` that divides **n** evenly (test with `n % d == 0`). Loop `d` from 2 upward and `break` the instant you find one. Assume `n >= 2`; a prime's first divisor is itself.
~~~lua
first_divisor(15)  -- 3
first_divisor(13)  -- 13
~~~
exercises.starter:
function first_divisor(n)
  -- TODO: scan d from 2 up; return the first d that divides n
  return -1
end
exercises.solution:
function first_divisor(n)
  local found = n
  for d = 2, n do
    if n % d == 0 then
      found = d
      break
    end
  end
  return found
end
exercises.hint: for d = 2, n do if n % d == 0 then found = d; break end end — the loop reaches n itself for a prime.
exercises.lore: Pry at the number from the smallest end. The first crack is the one that matters.

exercises.title: STORM SEQUENCE
exercises.prompt:
Define **storm_steps(n)** that applies this rule until **n** reaches **1**, counting the steps: if `n` is even, set `n = n // 2`; otherwise set `n = 3 * n + 1`. Because you act before testing, use a `repeat ... until n == 1` loop. Return the number of steps. Assume `n >= 1`.
~~~lua
storm_steps(1)  -- 0   (already at 1)
storm_steps(8)  -- 3   (8 -> 4 -> 2 -> 1)
~~~
exercises.starter:
function storm_steps(n)
  local steps = 0
  if n == 1 then return 0 end
  -- TODO: repeat the even/odd rule until n == 1, counting steps
  return steps
end
exercises.solution:
function storm_steps(n)
  local steps = 0
  if n == 1 then return 0 end
  repeat
    if n % 2 == 0 then
      n = n // 2
    else
      n = 3 * n + 1
    end
    steps = steps + 1
  until n == 1
  return steps
end
exercises.hint: Guard n == 1 first, then repeat the if/else rule with steps = steps + 1, until n == 1.
exercises.lore: Feed any seed to the storm. It always spirals home to one — eventually.

exercises.title: CAPPED RUN
exercises.brief: Accumulate until a cap, then break.
exercises.prompt:
Define **run_until_cap(list, cap)** that walks the array **list** adding values to a running total, but stops the moment the total would **reach or exceed** `cap`. Return an array of the values actually taken (those added before hitting the cap). Use a numeric `for` and `break`.
~~~lua
local t = run_until_cap({ 3, 4, 10, 1 }, 8)
print(t[1], t[2])  -- 3  4   (3+4=7 ok; adding 10 hits 17 >= 8, so stop)
print(#t)          -- 2
~~~
exercises.solution:
function run_until_cap(list, cap)
  local taken = {}
  local total = 0
  for i = 1, #list do
    if total + list[i] >= cap then
      break
    end
    total = total + list[i]
    table.insert(taken, list[i])
  end
  return taken
end

tests.name: stops before cap
tests.code:
local t = run_until_cap({ 3, 4, 10, 1 }, 8)
assert(#t == 2 and t[1] == 3 and t[2] == 4)

tests.name: takes all when under cap
tests.code:
local t = run_until_cap({ 1, 1, 1 }, 100)
assert(#t == 3 and t[3] == 1)

exercises.hint: Track a running total; for each item, if total + list[i] >= cap then break, else add it and table.insert.
exercises.lore: Pull data until the buffer's about to blow, then cut the line clean.
